main_summary.summary: Kay Tillow, born 1942 in Paducah, Kentucky and raised in Metropolis, Illinois, recounts her path from a non-political German-American farm family into Civil Rights Movement organizing and lifelong labor activism. Her Presbyterian minister Maynard Elftman gave her Anne Braden's The Wall Between as a high school student in the late 1950s. At the University of Illinois she joined the NAACP, walked her first picket line, and traveled with the local chapter to SNCC demonstrations in Cairo, Illinois, where she met Mary McCollum. A junior year abroad in Ghana in 1962 introduced her to W.E.B. Du Bois and Conor Cruise O'Brien. After the March on Washington she joined a delegation to Atlanta, met Prathia Hall and James Forman, then went to Hattiesburg, Mississippi during the voter registration push, where Bob Moses had been jailed. She left school to organize with the Appalachian Committee for Full Employment among Eastern Kentucky coal miners, met Carl and Anne Braden and James Dombrowski at Highlander Folk School, and met her husband Walter Tillow there. From 1966 she organized for the United Electrical, Radio and Machine Workers; from 1969 for National Union of Hospital and Health Care Employees, Local 1199 in Pennsylvania. She later led CLUW in Pittsburgh and now organizes the All Unions Committee for Single Payer Health Care behind John Conyers's HR 676.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Tillow's testimony documents the labor-civil rights bridge that connected SNCC-era southern organizing to the long arc of working-class movement-building in Appalachian Kentucky, Pittsburgh, and the rural Pennsylvania hospital campaigns of 1199's expansion. Her recollections of the Cairo, Illinois SNCC demonstrations, the Hattiesburg, Mississippi voter registration push, the March on Frankfort (March 5, 1964), and the Jewish Home and Hospital sit-in on Christmas Eve 1969 add ground-level texture to under-documented theaters of the movement.

The interview also preserves a first-person account of how Carl and Anne Braden mentored a generation of young SNCC workers through Highlander and The Southern Patriot, and how the Bradens insisted SNCC refuse the liberal anti-communism of Hubert H. Humphrey. Tillow's testimony links Medicare's 1965 hospital-desegregation mechanism to her contemporary work for HR 676, situating universal healthcare as an unfinished civil rights project.

chapters.title: Metropolis Childhood and Anne Braden's Book
chapters.summary: Tillow describes her birth in Paducah, Kentucky in 1942, growing up in Metropolis, Illinois in a German-American farm family of modest means. Her Presbyterian minister Maynard Elftman gave her Anne Braden's The Wall Between in high school, exposing her to the Wade case in Louisville and the sedition charge against Carl and Anne Braden.

chapters.title: University of Illinois NAACP
chapters.summary: Tillow recounts integration of Metropolis schools, nominating an African-American student for a high school girls' club and being rebuffed by the teacher. At the University of Illinois she joined the NAACP and walked her first picket line against a Champaign, Illinois store that refused to hire African-Americans.

chapters.title: Cairo and the Ghana Year
chapters.summary: In summer 1962 Tillow traveled with the local NAACP to SNCC demonstrations in Cairo, Illinois, where she met Mary McCollum, who had a deep gash from a demonstration. She then spent a junior year abroad in Ghana, witnessed W.E.B. Du Bois receive an honorary degree, and learned about decolonization from Conor Cruise O'Brien's To Katanga and Back.

chapters.title: Atlanta Delegation and Hattiesburg
chapters.summary: Back at the University of Illinois in fall 1963, Tillow joined her NAACP chapter's semester-break delegation to Atlanta, where she heard Prathia Hall and James Forman. The group traveled to Hattiesburg, Mississippi during voter registration demonstrations. Bob Moses had been jailed, and a white movement worker had been put in with white prisoners and beaten beyond recognition.

chapters.title: Highlander and the Bradens
chapters.summary: Tillow describes meeting Walter Tillow at Highlander Folk School around 1964, where she encountered Carl and Anne Braden and James Dombrowski. The Bradens shared The Southern Patriot and mentored young SNCC workers, drawing connections to the 1930s Southern Conference for Human Welfare. Tillow credits Carl Braden with persuading SNCC to refuse the liberal anti-communism of Hubert H. Humphrey.

chapters.title: Eastern Kentucky Coal Miners
chapters.summary: Tillow worked through 1964 with the Appalachian Committee for Full Employment among Eastern Kentucky coal miners who had been jailed on a federal conspiracy charge for trying to revive the miners' union. She edited a newspaper she recalls as Kentucky Jobs with Justice and joined the March on Frankfort, Kentucky for Jobs and Freedom on March 5, 1964.

chapters.title: Local 1199 in Pennsylvania
chapters.summary: Tillow describes leaving the United Electrical, Radio and Machine Workers in 1969 to work for National Union of Hospital and Health Care Employees, Local 1199. She organized in Lewistown, Wilkes-Barre, Spangler, Huntingdon, Canonsburg and other rural Pennsylvania hospitals, winning units that were majority-white against employer campaigns that branded 1199 a black union. Coretta Scott King supported the Johns Hopkins campaign in Baltimore.

chapters.title: Pittsburgh Hospital Strikes
chapters.summary: Tillow describes the 1969-70 1199 campaign in Pittsburgh where the National Labor Relations Act did not yet cover hospital workers. After the 1970 Presbyterian Hospital strike, Governor Milton Shapp's collective-bargaining legislation added hospital workers, opening Pennsylvania to organizing. A Christmas Eve 1969 sit-in at the Jewish Home and Hospital, led by Henry Nicholas, won a secret-ballot election overseen by a rabbi from Rodef Shalom Congregation.

chapters.title: Family, FBI, and Movement Foundations
chapters.summary: Tillow recalls her parents' opposition to her leaving school before graduation and their distress when the FBI visited them about her anti-war activity. She situates the anti-war movement, labor organizing, and civil rights work as a single moral project, citing Fannie Lou Hamer at the 1964 Democratic convention and Diane Nash's response to the Justice Department about the Freedom Rides.

chapters.title: CLUW and Pay Equity
chapters.summary: Tillow served as president of the Pittsburgh chapter of the Coalition of Labor Union Women, founded at the March 23-24, 1974 convention in Detroit. She describes the difficulty of getting recognition at the Allegheny County Central Labor Council and how CLUW chapters became focal points for women raising civil rights, anti-war, and union issues.

chapters.title: HR 676 and Single Payer
chapters.summary: Tillow describes her current work on the All Unions Committee for Single Payer Health Care behind John Conyers's HR 676, which would remove the for-profit insurance industry from healthcare. She cites Adewale Troutman and David Satcher on 83,570 excess deaths in the African American community attributable to healthcare disparities, and notes that the 1965 Medicare law desegregated southern hospitals by conditioning payment on integration.

chapters.title: Voting Rights and Movement Legacy
chapters.summary: Tillow connects the threats to voting rights from Shelby County v. Holder to the unfinished labor and healthcare agenda. She reflects on what she gained from the movement, the importance of expanding Social Security and Medicare, and the use of anti-immigrant politics to divide working people. She closes by emphasizing that movements are built from many ordinary people doing their parts.

loc_video.caption: Kay Tillow oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Louisville, Kentucky, 2013 August 14
